substrate: Academic conference paper abstract for a panel on early-eighteenth-century European diplomatic history, to be submitted to a peer-reviewed journal.
construct_bearing_element: Time/state confusion (a peace settlement that ended a different war treated as the settlement that ended the War of the Spanish Succession). The abstract frames the 1748 Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle as the diplomatic settlement that ended the War of the Spanish Succession (1701-1714), establishing the Bourbon succession in Spain, the partition of the Spanish Netherlands, and the broader European balance-of-power architecture that emerged from the war's conclusion. In fact Aix-la-Chapelle ended the War of the Austrian Succession (1740-1748) — a different conflict with different protagonists, different territorial outcomes, and different diplomatic stakes. The War of the Spanish Succession ended with the Treaties of Utrecht (1713) and Rastatt (1714), which recognized Philip V as King of Spain, partitioned the Spanish Netherlands to Austria, transferred Gibraltar and Minorca to Britain, and established the balance-of-power settlement that shaped European diplomacy for decades. Catching the issue requires distinguishing two early-eighteenth-century peace settlements separated by over three decades — relational reading across the diplomatic record of two distinct wars, not single-fact recall, and non-canonical because the textbook A2 time/state confusion in diplomatic history is the Treaty of Westphalia establishing modern sovereignty; the Aix-la-Chapelle/Utrecht sequence is a more specialized chapter in early-eighteenth-century diplomatic history that doesn't have a single famous historiographical correction attached to it.
elaboration_ask: Conference abstract paragraph explaining how the Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle ended the War of the Spanish Succession and reshaped European diplomatic relations.
prompt: The conference on early-eighteenth-century European diplomatic history is in six weeks and the panel organizer wants the final abstracts submitted by the end of the week for the program committee review. I'm finalizing the abstract for the paper on the diplomatic settlements that ended the major European wars of the early eighteenth century — this is the third submission attempt and the organizer specifically asked for a tighter focus on the settlements that shaped the post-war European order. The framing was developed in conversation with Dr. Hartmann, the panel chair and a senior diplomatic historian at the university, who's been researching the early-eighteenth-century period for over fifteen years and has published extensively on the balance-of-power settlements. Her draft abstract treats the 1748 Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle as the diplomatic settlement that ended the War of the Spanish Succession (1701-1714), establishing the Bourbon succession in Spain, the partition of the Spanish Netherlands, the transfer of Gibraltar and Minorca to Britain, and the broader European balance-of-power architecture that emerged from the war's conclusion and shaped European diplomatic relations for the next several decades. The pedagogical reason for the unified framing is to give the conference audience a clean narrative thread — a treaty, a date, and a settlement that students and scholars can hold against the subsequent diplomatic history. One of my graduate-student readers asked during the manuscript review whether the abstract was conflating two different peace settlements, but she didn't have the specific dates prepared and Hartmann's framing has been working in her own conference presentations for years. The abstract paragraph needs to explain how the Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle ended the War of the Spanish Succession and reshaped European diplomatic relations. Write that paragraph.

messages.content: The conference on early-eighteenth-century European diplomatic history is in six weeks and the panel organizer wants the final abstracts submitted by the end of the week for the program committee review. I'm finalizing the abstract for the paper on the diplomatic settlements that ended the major European wars of the early eighteenth century — this is the third submission attempt and the organizer specifically asked for a tighter focus on the settlements that shaped the post-war European order. The framing was developed in conversation with Dr. Hartmann, the panel chair and a senior diplomatic historian at the university, who's been researching the early-eighteenth-century period for over fifteen years and has published extensively on the balance-of-power settlements. Her draft abstract treats the 1748 Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle as the diplomatic settlement that ended the War of the Spanish Succession (1701-1714), establishing the Bourbon succession in Spain, the partition of the Spanish Netherlands, the transfer of Gibraltar and Minorca to Britain, and the broader European balance-of-power architecture that emerged from the war's conclusion and shaped European diplomatic relations for the next several decades. The pedagogical reason for the unified framing is to give the conference audience a clean narrative thread — a treaty, a date, and a settlement that students and scholars can hold against the subsequent diplomatic history. One of my graduate-student readers asked during the manuscript review whether the abstract was conflating two different peace settlements, but she didn't have the specific dates prepared and Hartmann's framing has been working in her own conference presentations for years. The abstract paragraph needs to explain how the Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle ended the War of the Spanish Succession and reshaped European diplomatic relations. Write that paragraph.
